narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 51.6% chance that you would not pass—tipping just over the threshold that leads to rejection for many applicants. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, family background, and enrollment plans, focusing on patterns seen in thousands of past students without considering gender or racial background. The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this failure prediction were your Year 1 grade decile, your LSAT score, and your Year 3 grade decile, in that order of influence. Your placement in the bottom decile for Year 1 grades stood out strongly as a red flag, as it placed you far below the top-half positioning typical of students who passed the bar—averaging around the 6th decile—suggesting early struggles that often carry forward. This was the single strongest driver toward the failure prediction. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 33.5 pulled things back somewhat, countering the risk since it was closer to the 37.3 average among bar passers, showing your potential in a key admissions metric. However, your Year 3 decile of 2 didn't provide enough lift, as it remained well below the near-top-half average of about 6 for successful students, reinforcing concerns about consistent performance. Compared to typical admitted students—who often hail from upper-middle or high family incomes (over half of them), boast stronger undergraduate GPAs around 3.24, and enroll full-time like your plans—your middle-income background and 2.9 undergrad GPA aligned somewhat with passers but couldn't offset the decile weaknesses. Your full-time status matches the norm for those who succeed, which is encouraging. While this outcome stings, especially given your dedication to pursuing law full-time, it highlights where targeted improvements could shift the odds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push toward 37 or higher, bolstering your undergrad record through post-bac courses if possible, and focusing on climbing grade deciles in any further studies—many students in similar spots have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps before reapplying. You're clearly motivated, and with these steps, a future application could look much stronger to the model and admissions team.
